ThinkPHP Day 6: Installing ThinkPHP with Composer

From this post on we finally get into the main event — the ThinkPHP chapters. But we're not going to jump straight into writing ThinkPHP code. First let's talk about how to install ThinkPHP through Composer (a PHP package manager, kind of like NPM for PHP). And of course we'll tweak ThinkPHP a little along the way too.
Back in the intro chapter I mentioned we'd be using Composer to install ThinkPHP, so the first thing we need to do is head over to the Composer official site and install Composer itself.
I already have it installed, and honestly the install process is dead simple (just keep clicking Next), so I won't go through it again here.

folder and dump them in there — both are totally fine.

After saving, you don't even need to restart the project — just hit F5 to refresh the page and you'll see the result.
Finally, let's poke around and see what else default ThinkPHP gives us. Out of the box, ThinkPHP also sets up two routes for us:
For the first route, if you go to
you'll see a nice short one-liner.
, you can customize how it greets you — and this is what's commonly called a
(more on that later). For example, you could go to
And you'll see the page greet you based on the route you typed in.
If you'd rather not install through Composer, you can also grab it from the ThinkPHP official site (though you'll need to log in to download).
Personally though, I'd recommend downloading it from the official GitHub repo instead.

We've finally gotten into something that actually feels like ThinkPHP. Back in the day when I was developing with ThinkPHP, I always installed it by downloading it manually. Going through this whole exercise, I actually learned along the way that ThinkPHP can be installed via Composer too.
In the next post, we'll take a look at ThinkPHP's directory structure.
